The traditional clothing of this group consists of fur garments that they make themselves, with hoods to protect them from the ice, sleeves and pockets.  In addition, their typical clothing includes seal gut raincoats, goggles to protect the eyes and snowshoes.

136.name: Eastern Siberian Native
136.overview:
Of Siberia's 40 million inhabitants, 10% are indigenous. Although some indigenous people, such as the Yakut, have their own republics, many others are in danger of extinction, threatened by the environmental degradation of the region.
The Yakut, included in this group, are the largest indigenous group in Siberia. They live in the Sakha Republic and number just over 450,000 individuals. Their main activity is hunting, fishing and horse breeding and, for the most part, they are Orthodox Christians.
The Even and the Ulchi are other indigenous communities of the region formed by 19,000 and 3,000 people, respectively, dedicated especially to reindeer breeding, hunting and fishing. In all of them it is common to use sleds to move through the snow.

147.name: Papuan
147.overview:
The Papuan people are the native ethnic group of Papua New Guinea. The country of Papua New Guinea comprises the eastern half of the island of New Guinea, as well as numerous surrounding islands. It is located in the region known as Melanesia, which in turn is part of Oceania.
Despite having only 7 million inhabitants, Papua New Guinea is considered to be one of the most culturally diverse countries in the world, with more than 1,000 distinct cultural groups, including more than 800 languages. Papua New Guinea has been populated since very remote times. The oldest archaeological remains found in the area date from 60,000 years ago. Although very little is known about these early settlers of Papua New Guinea, it is believed that they came from Southeast Asia. The descendants of the earliest settlers are known to have historically occupied the mountainous areas of the country while the coastal areas are home to descendants of later ancient migrations originating from Malaysia.

149.name: Melanesian
149.overview:
Melanesians are the indigenous inhabitants of the region of Melanesia, a sub-region of Oceania stretching from New Guinea in the west to Tonga in the east and including Fiji, Vanuatu, Solomon Islands and Papua New Guinea.
People in this region have a distinctive ancestry. Studies indicate that the first settlers in the region arrived during the migration of Africans to Asia between 50 000 and 100 000 years ago. Much later, between 4000 and 3000 BC, an influx of Austronesian migrants arrived in Melanesia. According to historians, there was a long period of interaction between the latter and the pre-existing inhabitants, resulting in numerous complex genetic, linguistic and cultural mixtures.

157.name: Maghrebi
157.overview:
The Maghreb is a region located in North Africa that includes the westernmost part of the Arab world. The region includes Algeria, Libya, Mauritania, Morocco, Western Sahara and Tunisia, as well as the autonomous cities of Ceuta and Melilla. It has a population of more than 87 million people.
The original inhabitants of the Maghreb were the Imazighen, generally called Berbers. With the arrival of the Arabs in the 7th century, the population of the Maghreb converted to Islam and began a long process of linguistic and cultural Arabization. The region has hosted several migratory waves, such as the arrival of the Andalusians, the Ottoman Empire or the French colonization, which have left their mark on the different cultures.
Today, most of the inhabitants of the Maghreb speak Maghrebi Arabic, although there is also a percentage of inhabitants who speak the Berber languages, French or Spanish.

162.name: West African (Gambian and Senegalese)
162.overview:
Senegal and Gambia are the two West African countries located furthest west on the continent. The former, with more than 15 million inhabitants, is named after the Senegal River that marks its northern border. The second, with a much smaller area and just over 2 million inhabitants, is completely surrounded by Senegal and situated on the banks of the Gambia River.
Both countries share a past strongly associated with the slave market, first by the Portuguese and later by the British Empire. In addition, much of their customs and culture are also shared. Throughout the region, the majority religion is Islam. They are also known for their excellent music and typical dances.
In the area, there are a number of endemic ethnic groups, the Mandinka being the most numerous, representing more than 30% of the population of both countries. Their population is over 13 million and their main language is the Manninka, with numerous dialects. They are predominantly Muslim, but very few dress in the Arab style and few women wear veils. Most of them are now farmers or fishermen and are highly esteemed jalis or bards, who are traditional storytellers who keep the history of the people alive.

164.name: West African (Sierra Leonean and Liberian)
164.overview:
Sierra Leone and Liberia are two West African countries founded to repatriate enslaved and subsequently freed people to the African continent in the 19th century. During this period, freed slaves founded Freetown, the capital of Sierra Leone. Due to the British influence in both countries, the official and most widely spoken language is English.
This region, with more than 10 million inhabitants, is one of the least developed in the world due to several social problems, although it has important mineral and fishing resources and an agriculture to be promoted. Nearly two thirds of its inhabitants live from agriculture and industry is limited to the processing of raw materials. The culture of both countries has a mixture of African and English elements.
There are several ethnic groups in the region, the Mandé people being the most numerous. They represent about 30% of the total population. Their main activities are cattle raising and hunting, which they carry out in a little mechanized way. Their main language is Mendé and they are characterized by an interesting fact: their men are initiated early in the Poro society in which they are prepared to gain wisdom, responsibility and power for several years. During this period, members converse with each other using a secret language and passwords that only other members know.

166.name: West African (Nigerian and Ghanaian)
166.overview:
Nigeria and Ghana are located in West Africa, specifically in the Gulf of Guinea. The territory has historically been the subject of constant European expeditions, mainly by the Portuguese, British and Dutch for the trade of the large amount of gold in the area, which is why it is also known as the Gold Coast.
The region is home to more than 230 million people, making it one of the most populated areas on the African continent. Most of them belong to Islam, although the percentage of Protestants and Christians is also relevant. In this region there are more than 250 different ethnic groups, being the Esan and Yoruba peoples some of the most important.
The Yoruba people have about 58 million inhabitants and constitute 30% of Nigeria's population. Most of them speak the Yoruba language and are mainly Muslim or Christian, although they maintain many tenets of the traditional faith of their ancestors. They are also one of the most urbanized peoples in Africa, living in well-organized urban centers.

170.name: East African (Kenyan and Ugandan)
170.overview:
Kenya and Uganda are two East African countries, the former with 46 million inhabitants and the latter with 1.2 million. Both share Lake Victoria, the second largest freshwater lake in the world and an important source of wealth for the inhabitants of the area. The region is known for its rich biodiversity.
Due to its climate and natural resources, agriculture is the most important sector in the region with coffee as the main product. The culture in Kenya and Uganda is very diverse because of the coexistence of a multitude of ethnic groups, such as the Luyia and Luo.
The Luyia people, numbering more than 10 million people, account for 14% of the region's inhabitants and are the largest ethnic group in Kenya. Their language is Luhya, derived from the Bantu languages. Their culture includes polygamy, although it is less and less practiced.
The Luo represent more than 10% of the population of the area, with more than 7 million people, and they speak the Luo language as well as Swahili and English. As a curiosity, in this ethnic group the names traditionally given to children reflected aspects related to pregnancy or childbirth, such as the season of the year or the time of birth.

173.name: Pygmy and San hunter-gatherer
173.overview:
The term Pygmy is used to designate groups of people living in Central Africa, especially in the African equatorial forests, who are characterized by their short stature. Together with the Bushmen or San, they are considered the oldest inhabitants of Africa. The latter are mostly located in the South of the continent.
The Pygmies hunt in a very traditional way, with nets, arrows and javelins, and gather fruits, tubers and honey. They speak different languages, although they share some words, which indicates that in the past they may have had a common language. The inbreeding practiced among them and their isolation in forests has caused a different evolution from the rest of the African peoples. For this reason, there are clear elements common to all of them: their short stature and their short life expectancy of between 15 and 24 years.
The Bushmen or San are also traditionally hunter-gatherers and speak Khoisan languages, characterized by the incorporation of clicking sounds. They are composed of several villages and have a population of about 105,000 people. Their society has been extensively studied and in it, women play an important role, with an elevated status from which they can make important decisions.

188.name: Iberian
188.overview: The Iberians are the original inhabitants of the Iberian Peninsula, in southwestern Europe, including the countries of Andorra, Portugal and Spain. Although the term Iberian has been used to identify all the inhabitants of the peninsula, there is another more restricted ethnic sense that identifies them as the ethnic group that occupied the east and south of the Iberian Peninsula. These peoples, defined by the Greeks and Romans, would have been influenced by other Mediterranean peoples such as the Phoenicians or the Greeks themselves.It is known that the first settlers arrived in the Iberian Peninsula more than 40,000 years ago from the south of France. From that point and throughout history the Iberian Peninsula has been a crossroads for many ethnic groups from the Mediterranean Sea, Central and Eastern Europe, which is reflected in the great cultural diversity of its current populations. In addition, due to the role played by Spain and Portugal since the end of the 15th century, at the time of the discoveries and subsequent colonization of America, a large proportion of the inhabitants of today's South American countries are descendants of the original Iberian Peninsula population.

192.name: Basque
192.overview:
The term French Basque refers to the group of people originating in northern Spain and southwest of France, located on both sides of the western Pyrenees. The origin of the Basque has given rise to many theories, some of which trace their origins to the first paleolithic settlers of Europe. In this sense, they have been related to other European and Mediterranean populations such as the Iberians, Picts, Irish, Welsh, Berbers or Etruscans.
The Basques are characterized by the Basque language that is not related to any of the Indo-European languages. Thanks to numerous genetic studies, it is thought that the Basques are a remnant of the first inhabitants of Western Europe; specifically, those of the Franco-Cantabrian region, which mixed with the first groups of Neolithic farmers arriving in the Iberian Peninsula.

193.name: Italian
193.overview:
Refers to the original population of the Italian Peninsula, located in southern Europe, which occupies a central position in the Mediterranean. The Italian population is estimated at about 60 million inhabitants. Due to what is known as the Italian diaspora that took place in different waves between the end of the 19th century and the middle of the 20th century, in which there was a massive movement of Italians to different areas of the world. It is estimated that today more than 80 million people worldwide have a direct or partial Italian ancestry.
Italy is the cradle of the Roman Empire, which from its beginnings in the 8th century BC until its demise in the 5th century dominated Western Europe and the Mediterranean area. This period, which left an indelible mark, still shapes the image of Western civilization. At the genetic level, today's Italians show genetic differences between their northern and southern populations. Southern Italians are closest to Mediterranean populations such as the Greeks, while northern Italians are closest to today's Iberians and southern French.

194.name: Sardinian
194.overview:
The Sardinian people refer to the inhabitants of the Italian island of Sardinia in the western Mediterranean. Researchers believe that the first settlers of Sardinia came from the Iberian Peninsula and the Italian peninsula. However, as with other isolated populations, today's Sardinians differ significantly from other European populations. In this sense, different investigations have shown how the Sardinian people show a clear genetic differentiation with respect to the rest of the populations of their geographical environment, since they present genetic variants considered rare in high frequencies.
Recent studies on the origin of the Sardinian people based on their genetic composition have shown that this ethnic group would be the closest to the first Neolithic populations, more than 10,000 years ago, who populated Europe from the Near East, displacing the preceding Paleolithic populations.

195.name: Greece and Balkan Peninsula
195.overview:
The Balkan peninsula, delimited to the north by the Balkan Mountains, is one of the three great peninsulas of southern Europe. The area, besides being surrounded by seas, has several rivers, such as the Danube, the Var or the Struma, which facilitate communication between the different regions.
The area comprises several countries such as Greece, Bulgaria, Croatia, Slovenia, Albania and part of Turkey, with a population of almost 53 million inhabitants. A multitude of different languages are spoken in the area, predominantly the Slavic language group (Bulgarian, Slovenian or Macedonian), Greek, and a group of Neo-Latin languages (such as Romanian or Moldavian). In addition, the heterogeneity of the area is also evident in the different cultures of its inhabitants.
The majority ethnic group in the area are the Greeks or Hellenes, with a population of more than 15 million people. They are native to Greece, Cyprus and some other Balkan regions, although, as a result of their enormous historical expansion, they constitute a significant diaspora, with Greek communities established all over the world. In ancient times, the Greeks were organized in city-states where the concept of democracy originated. Their main language is the Greek language, spoken since the time of Ancient Greece. It is an Indo-European language that constitutes a branch in itself and is closely related to Armenian and Indo-Iranian languages. This ethnic group is credited with very notable contributions to the field of universal culture, such as the first alphabet or important foundations in the field of philosophy.

208.name: Eastern European
208.overview:
Eastern Europe is the region of the European continent closest to Asia. There is no precise definition of the area it covers due to its different geographical, ethnic or social connotations. However, the region includes countries such as Russia, Poland, Estonia and Ukraine, the former being the largest and most populated and concentrating more than 15% of the total European population.
The region is characterized by Slavic, Greek, Byzantine and Ottoman cultural influences, which have contributed to its cultural heterogeneity. The region is made up of people belonging to dozens of ethnic groups such as Poles, Slovaks, Bosnians or Russians, among others, but with common cultural and/or genetic connections.

216.name: British and Irish
216.overview:
The British Isles, which according to some sources and experts include Great Britain and Ireland, are located to the northeast of the European coast. The British are descendants of various ethnic groups that settled in the area over the past millennia.
The British form a diverse, multinational and multicultural society. Thanks to genetic analyses carried out on ancient samples, it has been shown that the prehistoric native populations underwent a series of demographic events that led to their almost total replacement. It is worth highlighting the replacement of the Mesolithic populations that took place around 6000 BC, with the arrival of Neolithic farming populations. In addition to their prehistoric roots, modern Britons are known to be descendants of several ethnic groups that settled in the islands at different times. These ethnic groups are the Celts, Anglo-Saxons, Vikings and Normans.
Mainly due to the important expansion during the 19th and 20th centuries, the British population is widely distributed throughout the world, with an important presence in the United States, Australia, Canada, New Zealand and South Africa.

218.name: Orcadian
218.overview:
The Orcadians are an indigenous people inhabiting the Orkney Islands, an archipelago located in the north of Scotland with about seventy islands, of which only twenty are inhabited. The population is 22,200, of whom about 17,000 live on the largest island. Historically, the Orcadians are descendants of the Picts, Norse and Scots, and speak their own dialect derived from Scots and belonging to the Indo-European languages. The Vikings made Orkney their headquarters during their pirate expeditions.
The Orcadians base their economy on agriculture and marine resources. As for their religion, they practice Presbyterianism. They have a rich heritage of folklore.

219.name: Scandinavian
219.overview:
Scandinavia is a geographical and cultural region of northern Europe that includes Denmark, Sweden and Norway. The area is home to about 21 million people and its inhabitants speak the Nordic languages, although there are indigenous Saami-speaking minorities of Uralic origin.
Scandinavia has one of the world's most prosperous economies and is oriented towards international trade and exports. The region has undergone important migratory processes, with a strong emigration at the end of the First World War and an important arrival of immigrants after the Second World War, mainly from Finland, Iraq, Somalia, Germany and Syria.
The Scandinavian countries share much of their culture. They are strongly linked to the history of the Vikings, Germanic warriors from Scandinavia who were characterized by being great navigators, famous for their plundering and for leaving an important mark on the European history.

226.name: Finnish
226.overview:
Finns are the inhabitants of Finland, although today this ethnic group is not limited to this northern European country. The term Finnish also includes some populations of Sweden, such as the Tornedalians; from Norway, such as the Kven or Forest peoples; and even the Ingrians, from Russia.
There are currently approximately 7 million Finns, counting both the population that identifies with the ethnic group and their descendants. Most of them live in Finland and surrounding countries such as Sweden, Norway and Russia, however, due to different migratory processes, there are many relevant populations of Finns descendants in America and Oceania, in countries such as the United States, Canada, Australia, New Zealand, and even in Brazil and Argentina.

227.name: French
227.overview:
The French people are an ethnic group belonging to the Romance-speaking population group that originated in present-day France. Historically, the French people are predominantly of Gallic or Celtic, Latin and Germanic origin, but also with influences from the Basque, Iberian, Italic or Greek peoples, among others.
France has long been a mixture of local customs and regional differences, which is reflected in the language. Although most of them speak French, a language belonging to the Indo-European language family, others such as Occitan, Catalan, Corsican, Basque or Alsatian are spoken in their respective regions.
The French population is estimated at 95 million people. Of these, some 67 million reside in France, although descendants can also be found widely distributed in other countries such as the United States, Algeria, Belgium, Burkina Faso, Cameroon, Canada, Germany or Morocco, among many others. The majority of the French people belong to the Catholic Church, although Protestant, Jewish or Muslim representatives can also be found, as well as atheists or agnostics.

232.name: German
232.overview:
Germans are a Germanic ethnic group native to Central Europe. Although the estimate of the number of Germans depends on the criteria applied, it is estimated that the population would be around 150 million people, of which 62.5 million reside in Germany, which is 42% of its total population. However, the Germanic people are widely distributed around the globe. Most importantly, the United States is home to more than 60 million Germans, one third of the German diaspora. Other important locations are Brazil, Canada, Argentina, South Africa and France.
Their language is German, the second most spoken Germanic language after English. It belongs to the Indo-European language family. As for religion, at present one third of the population is Protestant, another third Roman Catholic and the remaining third is non-religious. Germanic society has had great contributions to society in multiple fields such as science, philosophy or literature.

239.name: Ashkenazi Jew
239.overview:
It refers to the Ashkenazi Jews, descendants of the Jews who settled in Central and Eastern Europe during the medieval period, mainly in Germany, Austria, Hungary, Czech Republic, Ukraine and Romania, among others. They developed particular customs and laws that differentiate them from other Jewish peoples, such as their own language, Yiddish, which combines terms from German dialects with some of Slavic or Hebrew origin.
It is estimated that the Ashkenazi population amounts to 11.2 million people, and some researchers indicate that they represent 70% of the Jew population of the world. They have a large diaspora, most significantly in the United States (5-6 million people) and Israel (2.8 million), but also in Russia, Argentina, the United Kingdom, Canada, etc.
Its history is marked by the Nazi Holocaust during World War II, in which two thirds of Ashkenazi Jews were murdered, also causing a massive migration to other regions.

243.name: Egyptian, Levantine and Arabian
243.overview:
The Arabian Peninsula and Egypt are located at the confluence of Africa and Asia. Because of their geographical proximity, both regions share important cultural and social aspects, with a common majority language, Arabic.
Egypt borders the Mediterranean to the north and the Red Sea to the east. It is mainly a desert region, but is crossed by the Nile River, which has provided the country with very fertile, densely populated areas and has marked the rich history of the region. Egypt was the cradle of the ancient Egyptian civilization and, together with the Mesopotamian civilization, was the origin of today's Western culture, decisively influencing the history of mankind. Different ethnic groups coexist in the country, with the Egyptians being the majority group with more than 100 million inhabitants, followed by the Bedouins, Gypsies and Nubians, with more than 1 million people each.
The Arabian Peninsula and the Mediterranean Levant include countries such as Saudi Arabia, the United Arab Emirates, Kuwait, Oman, Qatar, Yemen, Syria, Lebanon and Palestine, among others. It is generally a largely desert area, although the presence of monsoons in the south and east allow for agricultural activities. Arab identity should not be confused with religious affiliation, although most Arabs today are Muslims, with small Christian minorities.

253.name: Turkish, Caucasian and Iranian
253.overview:
Turkey, Iran and the Caucasus are located in Western Asia and include more than 183 million people.
The Caucasus, named after a mountain range, includes Azerbaijan, Armenia and Georgia. The people currently residing in this area speak about 100 different languages and dialects, most of which belong to the Caucasian language family. It is one of the most ethnically diverse regions in the world where some groups, such as the Adyghe, have been present for thousands of years and others, such as the Russians, arrived a few centuries ago. It is an ecologically rich area with a wealth of mineral and energy resources. It has a rich folkloric tradition with many connections to ancient Indian, Scandinavian and Greek cultures.
The majority ethnic group in Turkey is the Turkic people, although their presence is not limited to this country. This area, due to its strategic position, has been a historical crossroads between different cultures and civilizations, which is reflected in its heterogeneous culture.
Iran, whose official language is Persian, is home to the Iranian peoples, a group of ethnic groups that share the Iranian languages and originate from the Iranian plateau. Although the majority of Iranian peoples live in Iran, they are widely distributed with significant presence also in other regions of East and West Asia, with an estimated total population of about 210 million people.

258.name: Bedouin
258.overview:
The name Bedouin is attributed to the nomadic Arab population that inhabits the deserts of the Near East, the Arabian Peninsula and North Africa. Their origin is established in the Arabian Peninsula, although during the Arab conquests of the 7th century they expanded widely throughout the rest of the regions. It is estimated that the Bedouin population amounts to more than 25 million people settled mainly in Sudan, where they are the majority ethnic group, followed by Algeria, Saudi Arabia and Iraq, among other countries.
Today's Bedouins are generally organized into tribes. Their main language is Bedawi or Bedauye, a language of Afro-Asiatic origin widely spoken in the vicinity of the Red Sea. Their economy is centered on cattle raising and they organize their life around this activity. They are constantly on the move in search of water and pasture. As for their religion, most of them are Sunni Muslims.

276.name: Central Asian
276.overview:
Central Asia consists of five countries: Kazakhstan, Kyrgyzstan, Tajikistan, Turkmenistan and Uzbekistan. It includes more than 71 million people, although it is one of the least densely populated regions. Central Asia has historically been characterized by its nomadic peoples and its location on the Silk Road. The aridity of the area and its remoteness from the sea have made it difficult to practice agriculture and trade, the main reason for the nomadic nature of its peoples.
During the Middle Ages, the region was predominantly composed of Iranian peoples, with current representation in the area through the Pashtuns, Pamiris or Tajiks. After the expansion of the Turkic peoples, it also became the homeland of the Uzbeks, Kazakhs, Kyrgyz and Uyghurs and, as a result, this region is also called Turkestan.

281.name: Pakistani and Afghan
281.overview:
Pakistan and Afghanistan are located in South Asia. They have more than 200 million and 36 million inhabitants respectively.
The region has been, due to its geographical location, home to different cultures, as well as the object of invasions and settlements by Hindus, Persians, Greeks or Muslims, among others. Pakistan and Afghanistan are two countries characterized by their great linguistic and ethnic variety. Pakistan is home to a variety of peoples such as the Pashtuns, Sindhis, Punjabis or Baloch, whose official languages are Urdu and English. Afghanistan is inhabited by peoples such as the Pashtuns - who account for 42% of its population -, Tajiks, Hazaras, Uzbeks and Baloch, and they speak both the Pashto language and the Dari dialect.
The predominant religion is Islam, which is an important part of their culture and lifestyle.

283.name: Gujarati Indian
283.overview:
The Gujaratis are an ethnic group that traditionally inhabits the state of Gujarat, located in the southwest of India, bordering Pakistan to the northwest, the state of Rajasthan to the north, the state of Madhya Pradesh to the east, the state of Maharashtra to the southeast and the Arabian Sea to the West.  They speak Guyarati, one of the Indo-Aryan languages spoken in the Indian subcontinent.
The state of Guyarat has more than 60 million inhabitants, making it the ninth most populous state in India. Since becoming a state on May 1, 1960, Gujaratis has been one of the fastest growing economic areas in India. East is the birthplace of two of India's foremost independence leaders: Mahatma Gandhi and Sardar Vallabhbhai Patel.
In addition, the Gujaratis have a long seafaring tradition and a history of migration to different territories, mainly on the coasts of the Arabian Sea, with which they share the same mercantile culture. It is considered that the Gujaratis represent around 30% of the migrations of Indian populations. Currently, the main Guyarati colonies by number of inhabitants are in Pakistan, the United Kingdom, the United States and Canada, as well as in several countries in East and South Africa.

285.name: Punjabi
285.overview:
The Punjabis are an ethnolinguistic group associated with the Punjabi region, located in northern India and Pakistan. They are more than 145 million inhabitants with an important diaspora, especially in the United Kingdom, Canada and the United States.
Traditionally, the linguistic, geographical and cultural fusion created among the inhabitants of the state of Punjab an identity independent of their historical or religious origin. Its culture is one of the oldest in the world, although it is marked by the complexity of Hindu, Buddhist and Islamic influences, with special relevance in poetry, philosophy, spirituality and cuisine.

292.name: Bengali, Bangladeshi and Northeast Indian
292.overview:
Bengalis are one of the native Indo-Aryan ethnic groups of the Bengal region of South Asia, which is divided between Bangladesh and India. They make up 98% of the population of Bangladesh and their language is Bengali, a language of the Indo-European language family.
The Bengali population is estimated at 260 million people, of which about 163 million reside in Bangladesh, and can also be found in other regions such as India, Pakistan, Saudi Arabia, the United Arab Emirates, the United Kingdom and Qatar. The Bengalis represent, after the Han Chinese and Arabs, the third largest human ethnic group in terms of population.
Within the population, four different religious subgroups can be found: Muslims, who represent the majority, Hindus, Christians and Buddhists. In addition, like all major cultural groups in history, Bengalis have exerted a great influence in various fields, especially in arts and architecture, language, folklore, politics or technology.

308.name: Sri Lankan and Southern Indian
308.overview:
The Telugu people are an ethnic group native to the southern tip of the Indian subcontinent, south of the Narmada and Mahanadi rivers.  This region basically consists of the states of Andhra Pradesh, Tamil, Nadu, Karnataka and Kerala. The population of these regions amounts to more than 230 million inhabitants, about 20% of the country's population. They are also present in Sri Lanka, where they make up about 17% of the population.
The Telugu people speak the Telugu language, which belongs to the Dravidian languages, spoken by more than 200 million people in the Indian subcontinent. In addition to their language, the Dravidian peoples are distinguished from the other inhabitants of India by their physique, as they are generally short in stature and have dark brown skin.
The Sinhalese predominate in Sri Lanka (75% of its population), an ethnic group native to the island, characterized by speaking Sinhalese and are the result of the interbreeding of the natives with the Indo-Europeans who dominated the country. Sinhalese society is characterized by being mainly Buddhist and by the important role of women in society with an active presence in different areas.

poblation.AFR_PSH_KHS: San y Khoisan de África Austral
poblation.EAS_MON_MNS: Northeast Mongolia and Amur River Basin
poblation.OCE_PAP_SEP: Papuans of the Sepik River basin

overview.AFR_PSH: The term pygmy is used to designate individuals living in Central Africa, especially in the African equatorial forests. Together with the Bushmen or San, they are considered the oldest inhabitants of Africa. The high inbreeding of these tribes, together with their isolation in forests, has given them a very distinctive genetics with respect to the rest of African populations. As a curiosity, and as a result of such characteristic genetics, it is worth mentioning the short stature and short life expectancy (between 15-24 years) of the individuals that make up these tribes.
overview.AFR_PSH_BEM: Approximately 3,000 years ago, Bantu-speaking farmers originating from the region between Cameroon and Nigeria began a journey that would last millennia and lead them to populate most of sub-Saharan Africa, a process known as the Bantu Expansion. Taking with them their knowledge of agriculture and iron metallurgy, they expanded eastward to the Great Lakes region, and southward through the Congo rainforest. In their advance, these peoples encountered and interacted with the hunter-gatherers inhabiting the region and with Nilo-Saharan pastoralists in East Africa (such as the ancestors of the Luo and the Dinka), forging a genetic mosaic that reflects not only the Bantu origin, but also this rich history of exchange.
overview.AFR_PSH_BKA: The Aka, or Biaka, are a hunter-gatherer people of the Congo rainforest whose oral tradition has been recognized by UNESCO as a world heritage. This cultural recognition reflects their status as one of the oldest genetic lineages in the world. Their ancestors separated from other human populations very early in history, preserving a heritage that offers a direct window into the deep past of our species. This long history of continuity has shaped not only their DNA, but also their complex social structures, such as their cooperative parenting models and their polyphonic music, enriched in recent millennia by contact with neighboring farming peoples.
overview.AFR_PSH_KHS: The Khoisan peoples of Southern Africa, with a deep connection to the Kalahari Desert, are bearers of the oldest genetic lineages of humanity. Scientific evidence shows that their ancestors represent the earliest and deepest divergence in the family tree of modern humans, more than 150,000 years ago. This ancient legacy is reflected in their Khoisan languages, known for their distinctive click sounds, and in their profound knowledge as hunter-gatherers and pastoralists. For thousands of years, they were the predominant population in the region, until the arrival of Bantu-speaking farmers from the north, an event that transformed the demographic landscape of the southern continent. Therefore, this genetic profile connects with the history of resilience of the first peoples of Africa and with the deepest roots of all humanity.
overview.AFR_PSH_MBT: The Mbuti are hunter-gatherers of the Ituri rainforest, in the Congo Basin, and represent one of the oldest branches of humanity. Genetic evidence shows that the ancestors of the Mbuti separated from the ancestors of almost all other living human populations more than 100,000 years ago, preserving a lineage of extraordinary antiquity. Adapted for millennia to life in the equatorial rainforest, their history is also marked by interaction with Bantu agricultural peoples who arrived in the region more recently. This prolonged contact has produced a certain degree of genetic exchange, so that modern Mbuti, while maintaining their unique ancestral profile, also carry a small proportion of Bantu ancestry. Thus, their heritage is a testament to deep African history, a unique genetic profile complemented by rich cultural traditions.
overview.AMR_AMZ: This ancestry delves into the heart of the Amazon, connecting with the deep history of the indigenous peoples who have inhabited this vast and diverse region for millennia. Peoples such as the Karitiana and the Suruí, of the Tupí linguistic family in Brazil, present a history of relative isolation that has made their DNA a key scientific reference for understanding the ancestral history of the first inhabitants of South America. The genetic profile they represent is a pillar for identifying the heritage of the numerous peoples who adapted and expanded throughout the Amazon basin. Although each Amazonian people possesses a unique identity, they all share a deep ancestral connection that distinguishes them as one of the main branches in the genealogical tree of Native Americans.
overview.AMR_AND: Andean populations inhabit the Andes mountain range, the longest mountain range in the world, which crosses South America from north to south. Their ancestors, descendants of the first settlers who crossed the Bering Strait, established advanced civilizations such as the Inca, which left a deep genetic and cultural footprint in the region. The Quechua and Aymara are two of the largest indigenous peoples in the Andean region, although they share a common ancestor with other indigenous groups in America, they have had a degree of genetic isolation due to the mountainous topography of the region. Both groups are characterized by specific adaptations, such as the presence of certain genetic variants that are associated with a greater ability to transport oxygen in the blood, which have allowed them to adapt to extreme altitudes.
overview.AMR_AND_QUE: Natives of Aymara and Quechua origin are indigenous peoples who mainly inhabit the Andean region, in territories of Bolivia, Peru, Chile, and Argentina. With a rich cultural heritage, the Aymara and Quechua, including the Collas and Cachi peoples, have maintained their traditions for centuries, being known for their mastery of terrace agriculture and their deep connection with nature. Their history dates back to pre-Inca civilizations and their integration into the Inca empire, whose influences are still present in their language, clothing, music, and customs. These peoples have preserved their identity over time, significantly contributing to the cultural heritage of South America.
overview.AMR_AND_WIC: The Wichí mainly inhabit northern Argentina, primarily in the semi-arid tropical region of the Gran Chaco Americano. Traditionally, they have been a hunter-gatherer people, with a deep sense of identity, maintaining their own language and a close link with nature and the Chaco forest. Throughout their history, marked by resistance to various adversities, the Wichí have managed to preserve their traditions and culture, while adapting to the changes of the contemporary world. Their origins date back to the first human migrations in South America, making them one of the oldest ethnic groups in the region.
overview.AMR_CST_AND: Andean populations inhabit the Andes mountain range, the longest mountain chain in the world, which crosses South America from north to south. Their ancestors, descendants of the first settlers who crossed the Bering Strait, established civilizations like the Inca, which left a deep genetic and cultural footprint in the region. The Quechua and Aymara are two of the largest indigenous peoples of the Andean region, although they share a common ancestor with other indigenous groups of America, they have had a degree of genetic isolation due to the mountainous topography of the region. Their prolonged residence at high altitudes is also reflected in their biology, as many Andean populations possess unique genetic variants associated with greater efficiency in oxygen transport, a remarkable adaptation to life at extreme altitudes.
overview.AMR_CST_MES: Mesoamerica, one of the great cradles of world civilization, presents a deep shared history that connects dozens of native peoples, descendants of the humans who crossed the ancient Bering Land Bridge during the last Ice Age. It was in this region that corn was domesticated, a milestone that sustained the development of complex and flourishing societies for thousands of years, from the Olmecs to the Aztecs and Mayans. Despite its vast linguistic diversity, these peoples shared calendars, writing, and worldviews, creating not only a large cultural area, but also an interconnected genetic landscape. This legacy endures today in communities such as the Nahua, Maya, Zapotec, Mixtec, and many others.
overview.AMR_CST_ORI: The Orinoco River basin is one of the most biodiverse regions in Latin America, and it is home to a remarkable diversity of indigenous peoples, such as the Piapoco (also known as Wenaiwika), Piaroa, and Warao. These communities have inhabited the region for thousands of years, distributing themselves along the rivers that flow into the Orinoco, in what are today the territories of Venezuela and Colombia. The ancestral narratives of groups such as the Wenaiwika often describe important migrations and interactions with other peoples of the region, including movements from areas such as the Rio Negro basin. These ancestral journeys and exchanges have significantly contributed to weaving the rich and complex cultural tapestry that characterizes the Orinoco River basin.
overview.AMR_NTH: The Indigenous peoples of North America are descendants of the first humans who crossed the Beringia land bridge and diversified throughout the vast continent. This lineage is particularly representative of peoples such as the Pima (Akimel O`odham), whose ancestors have inhabited the deserts of Arizona and Sonora for millennia. They are considered the heirs of the sophisticated Hohokam culture, masters of irrigation who built hundreds of kilometers of canals to cultivate in the desert between 200 and 1500 AD.
overview.CSA_CAS: The heart of Eurasia, from the Central Asian steppes to the edges of Siberia, a region that has been a bridge and a meeting ground for peoples of the West and East for thousands of years. Genetically, its populations form a large continuum, where West Eurasian ancestry (linked to Bronze Age steppe herders and Iranian peoples) is strongest in the west, and East Asian ancestry is dominant in the east. This gradient is the result of opposing migrations: first, the expansion of Indo-Iranian peoples eastward, and later, the westward expansion of Turkic and Mongolian peoples from their homeland in East Asia. Consequently, this lineage connects the mixed heritage of peoples such as Uzbeks, Uyghurs, and Kazakhs to that of their Siberian neighbors, who represent the easternmost extreme of this ancient Eurasian continuum.
overview.CSA_PAF: Located in South Asia and with more than 235 million inhabitants, are Pakistan and Afghanistan. Due to their geographical location, they are a region that has been settled by a variety of peoples. For this reason, they are currently two countries with a high degree of genetic variability, inhabited by numerous ethnic groups. In Pakistan, the Pashtun, Sindhi, Punjabi or Baluchi populations stand out, while in Afghanistan, groups such as the Pashtuns, Yayikos, Hazaras, Uzbeks or Baluchis can be found.
overview.CSA_PAF_BAL: The Baloch are an Iranian-speaking people whose history is marked by a significant migration from the western Iranian plateau to their current homeland in Balochistan. Originating near the Caspian Sea, their ancestors began moving southeast approximately a millennium ago, adapting their nomadic culture to the new environment. Upon settling, they mixed with the pre-existing populations of the area, including Dravidian-speaking peoples. As a result, the Baloch genetic profile combines a strong ancestral component from western Iran, connecting them to other Iranian peoples like the Kurds, with significant South Asian influences, forging a unique identity through centuries of movement and adaptation.
overview.CSA_PAF_KAL: The Kalash represent a unique and extraordinarily well-preserved lineage from the Hindu Kush mountains in Pakistan. Due to their prolonged geographical and cultural isolation, the Kalash are considered a "genetic isolate," meaning that their DNA profile has experienced very little mixing with neighboring populations for thousands of years. Genetic studies suggest that the Kalash descend from an ancient indigenous population of the region, showing a strong affinity with Bronze Age steppe herders, likely the earliest Indo-Iranian peoples to arrive in the area. Thus, the Kalash are a testament to the deep history of the region, having managed to preserve their distinctive language, culture, and pre-Islamic religion through the centuries.
overview.CSA_PAF_PAF: The region encompassing present-day Pakistan and Afghanistan connects the Iranian plateau, Central Asia, and the Indian subcontinent. Its deepest roots lie with the builders of the Indus Valley Civilization, one of the world`s earliest major urban societies. The next defining chapter was the arrival of Indo-Iranian-speaking pastoralists from the Central Asian steppes in the Bronze Age, who mixed with the local population and established the lineages that prevail in the region to this day. Subsequently, the passage of Greeks, Kushans, and Turkic-Mongol peoples through the passes of the Hindu Kush added further layers to this profile, forging a complex genetic mosaic that unites the diverse peoples of this historic region.
overview.CSA_PUN: The state of Punjab, located in northwestern India, has acted as a major passageway for all major human invasions in the Indian subcontinent. The result has been the mixing of foreign genes with the local populations, resulting in a wide range of genetic diversity and influencing the genetic structure of the Punjab populations. Today the most representative ethnic group in the region are the Punjabis, a population highly influenced by Hindu, Buddhist and Islamic culture.
overview.CSA_PUN_BUR: The Burusho population is located in the heart of a fascinating linguistic and genetic enigma in the valleys of Hunza and Gilgit-Baltistan, in northern Pakistan. This people is renowned for its language, Burushaski, which is a "linguistic isolate" with no known relatives in the world, suggesting it is the last vestige of a formerly more widespread language family in the region. However, their genetic profile is not isolated; the Burusho share a deep common ancestry with their neighboring populations, the product of a rich history of intermixing between ancient lineages from Eurasia's steppes and from Western and Eastern Asia. Therefore, the history of the Burusho is an extraordinary lesson in cultural persistence, where a people managed to preserve its unique ancestral language while participating in the dynamic genetic exchange that has defined this part of the world.
overview.CSA_PUN_PUN: The Punjab region and the northwestern part of the Indian subcontinent form a historical region known as the "land of five rivers" that was both a cradle of civilizations and a gateway for ancient migrations. Its deepest genetic roots trace back to the builders of the Indus Valley Civilization, one of the world`s earliest urban societies. Subsequently, the arrival of Indo-European (Indo-Aryan) pastoralists from the Central Asian steppe during the Bronze Age introduced a new and decisive ancestral component. The fusion of these two major groups—the local Indus inhabitants and the steppe newcomers—was a pivotal event that shaped the genetic and cultural profile of North India and Pakistan, later enriched by the legacy of Persian and Central Asian empires.
overview.EAS_DAI_BSA: Insular Southeast Asia narrates one of the most extraordinary maritime expansions in human history: that of the Austronesian peoples. Genetic and linguistic evidence places the origin of this great migration on the island of Taiwan about 4,000 years ago, home to peoples like the Atayal and Ami. From there, they first expanded southward, reaching the Philippines and laying the foundations for populations like those of Luzon and the Visayas. Continuing their journey, another wave reached the vast island of Borneo, where their descendants formed diverse groups such as the Dusun and the Murut. Therefore, this genetic profile unites the indigenous peoples of Taiwan with those of the Philippines and Borneo, revealing a shared heritage that spread across the sea and shaped the human landscape of insular Southeast Asia.
overview.EAS_DAI_IBI: This group is located in the heart of mainland Southeast Asia and tells a story of successive waves of migration that overlapped to create the rich cultures of Burma (Myanmar), Thailand, and Cambodia. The oldest genetic base in the region was established by the first Austroasiatic-speaking rice farmers, whose legacy endures most strongly in the present-day Khmer (Cambodian) people. Later, new migrations from the north brought the Sino-Tibetan-speaking ancestors of the Burmese and the Tai-Kadai-speaking ancestors of the Thai, who mixed with the pre-existing Austroasiatic population. Added to this mixture was a profound cultural influence from India, resulting in a mosaic of peoples who, although genetically related, developed unique and distinctive identities and languages.
overview.EAS_DAI_IGO: The history of the Igorots dates back to the first Austronesian farmers who, in their expansion from Taiwan, arrived in the Philippine archipelago about 4,000 years ago. Their ancestors settled in the rugged mountains of the Cordillera, in northern Luzon, a geography that provided them with refuge and allowed them to resist subsequent colonial influences. As a result, the Igorot are considered one of the groups with the least external genetic admixture in the Philippines, making their DNA a direct window into the heritage of the archipelago's first Austronesian settlers. This deep and ancient legacy is manifested in their diverse Austronesian languages and in their impressive rice terraces, a millennia-old testament to their successful adaptation to mountain life.
overview.EAS_DAI_MAI: Malaysia and Indonesia are two island countries of Southeast Asia that share borders and an ancestral origin. The oldest genetic substrate of these islands comes from the first hunter-gatherers, related to the current Papuans. Around 4,000 years ago, the arrival of Austronesian-speaking farmers, originating from the expansion that departed from Taiwan, transformed the demographic landscape, mixing with and, to a large extent, replacing the pre-existing groups. Therefore, the genetic profile of Malays, Javanese, and other peoples of the region is primarily a mosaic of this dominant Austronesian heritage, with an older substrate and later layers of exchanges with India and China.
overview.EAS_DAI_SCI: Southern China and northern mainland Southeast Asia is a region transformed by the expansion of the world`s first rice farmers from the Yangtze River valley. The genetic profile of groups such as the Dai of southern China is highly representative of these ancient farmers, whose first major southward movement laid the foundations for peoples such as the Kinh of Vietnam. The region also became home to other groups, such as the Tibeto-Burman-speaking Lahu, who migrated to this area later and mixed with the pre-existing agricultural populations. This group, therefore, unites diverse peoples through a common ancestral legacy, forged in the cradle of rice cultivation and spread through the great waterways and valleys of Southeast Asia.
overview.EAS_HAN: The Han people are an ethnic group originating in China. It is one of the largest ethnic groups in the world, numbering approximately 1.2 billion people (about 18% of the world's population). Several scientific studies suggest that the Hans are descendants of the Huaxia tribe, an ancient population with which they share most genetic markers. It is now a very genetically homogeneous population.
overview.EAS_HAN_MIA: The origin of the Miao (also known as Hmong) is found in the ancient rice-farming peoples of the Yangtze River valley, in southern China, a region that was the cradle of many cultures. While many descendants of these farmers integrated into the growing Han civilization, the ancestors of the Miao forged a distinct path, often migrating to the mountainous regions of the southwest. This historical process explains why the Miao speak languages of the Hmong-Mien family, unrelated to Chinese, but share a genetic heritage with the populations of southern China.
overview.EAS_HAN_NHA: This group has its epicenter in the North China Plain, the cradle of Han civilization, and originates from the ancient peoples who domesticated millet in the Yellow River basin more than 8,000 years ago. The success of this agriculture allowed for a large demographic expansion, forming the core of the early Chinese dynasties and the genetic profile that we now associate with northern Han Chinese. This lineage expanded southwards over the centuries, profoundly influencing the populations of the Yangtze River valley. Therefore, this profile represents one of the foundational lineages of East Asia, a legacy that spread from the historical heartland of China to shape most of its present-day population.
overview.EAS_HAN_NYI: The Naxi and Yi peoples are indigenous to southwest China and are part of the larger Sino-Tibetan language family. The history of this family began thousands of years ago with the early millet farmers of northern China. From this ancestral cradle, one branch expanded to give rise to the Sinitic peoples (such as the Han), while another, the Tibeto-Burman branch, migrated to the mountains of Yunnan and Sichuan. The ancestors of the Naxi and Yi were part of this Tibeto-Burman migration, developing their unique cultures here, such as the Dongba pictographic script. Therefore, although the Naxi and Yi possess deeply distinctive identities, they share an ancient genetic origin with the Han peoples, reflecting a common history that diverged millennia ago.
overview.EAS_HAN_SHA: This group includes peoples located in the heart of southern China and has its deepest roots in the ancient peoples who led the rice cultivation revolution in the Yangtze River valley. The lineage of these farmers forms the main genetic component of the southern Han Chinese, including Cantonese speakers (Yue), and spread throughout the region along with the expansion of Han civilization. Peoples such as the She and the Tujia also descend from this same ancestral substratum; although they have retained distinct identities, their long history of coexistence with their Han neighbors has created a deep genetic affinity. Therefore, this group represents a large family of southern Chinese peoples who, despite their diverse cultural identities, share a common origin in the region's early farmers.
overview.EAS_MON: This group includes individuals of the Mongolian people and of the Oroquen, Xibe, Hezher and Daur ethnic groups, established in northern China. The Mongols are the majority ethnic group of this group and have approximately 10 million individuals, distributed in northern China, Mongolia and southern Asia. Given the common historical past shared by all these populations, they are closely related ethnic groups from a genetic point of view.
overview.EAS_MON_MCO: This group originates in the heart of the East Asian steppe, the historical homeland of nomadic pastoralist confederations that shaped the history of Mongolia and parts of northern China. Its deepest roots lie in ancient proto-Mongolic peoples such as the Xianbei; groups like the Monguor (or Tu) are considered direct descendants of these ancient populations. This lineage reached its greatest extent with the Mongol Empire, and includes neighboring peoples such as the Tungistic-speaking Xibo, who, despite their distinct language, share a deep regional genetic origin and a long history of interaction. Therefore, this genetic profile represents the heritage of the great equestrian empires of the eastern steppe, a legacy shared by Mongols and other closely related peoples who dominated this vast region.
overview.EAS_MON_MNS: In northeastern Mongolia, between the Khentii Mountains and the Siberian taiga, lives a predominantly Mongolian population that maintains a nomadic lifestyle based on pastoralism. These populations possess a significant genetic contribution from ancient inhabitants of the Amur River basin, whose genetic profile dates back to the ancient hunter-gatherers who populated this area since the Ice Age. This legacy is also carried by Tungusic-speaking peoples such as the Ulchi, Oroqen, and Hezhen, and by their Mongolic-speaking neighbors, such as the Daur, who, despite their different language, share the same deep local genetic root. Therefore, this heritage represents one of the oldest branches of the Northeast Asian family tree, a story of adaptation to the cold taiga forests that predates the expansion of the Mongol empires.
overview.EAS_SNT: Siberia is a region that extends across northern Asia and forms the most Asian part of Russia. It has approximately 40 million inhabitants, of which 10% are indigenous tribes. Among the most representative ethnic groups in the territory are the Yakut people, who share genetic similarities with other indigenous groups that inhabit the region, such as the Evenks and the Ulchi. In addition, and given the geographic location of these peoples, evolution has led to the prevalence of genetic modifications that have allowed them to adapt to extreme climates.
overview.EAS_SNT_SAB: In the heart of Siberia, we can find an extraordinary direct link to the hunter-gatherers who inhabited this vast region during the Ice Age. Peoples such as the Nenets, Nganasans, and Kets are direct carriers of one of the oldest genetic lineages in Eurasia, known as "Ancient North Eurasian" (ANE). This ancient Siberian lineage not only remained in the region, but also contributed crucially to the formation of other populations: it expanded westward to become part of European ancestry and eastward through Beringia to become a fundamental component of all Native American peoples. Therefore, this Siberian heritage is a living link connecting the ancestral histories of Europe and America through their common origin in ancient Siberia.
overview.EAS_SNT_SAC: Within this group are the native peoples of the extreme northeast of Siberia, the inhabitants of the Bering Sea coasts who are the closest Asian relatives of all the Indigenous peoples of the Americas. Their ancestors were part of a single population that lived during the Ice Age in Beringia, the ancient land bridge that connected Asia and America, and from which the first settlers of the American continent originated. While one branch of their ancient relatives crossed into the New World, the ancestors of the Siberian Yupik, Koryaks, and Itelmens remained in Siberia, adapting their marine hunting skills to the Arctic coasts. Therefore, this lineage not only represents the heritage of the resilient Arctic peoples of Siberia, but also constitutes a fundamental genetic bridge linking the ancestral histories of Asia and America.
overview.EAS_SNT_SME: Southern Siberia, centered on the crucial Altai-Sayan mountain region, was a crossroads that served as a cradle for the great nomadic peoples of the steppe. The genetic profile of the peoples of this area is a distinctive fusion of Northeast Asian lineages and the ancient hunter-gatherers of Siberia. It was from this ancestral admixture in the forest-steppe that the peoples who would later become known as Turkic and Mongolian arose; groups such as the Altai and Tuvan are representative of this origin in the heart of the Turkic world, while the Buryat and Kalmyk represent the Mongolian branch. Thus, this heritage unites diverse language families (Turkic, Mongolic, and Tungusic) through a shared origin as equestrian pastoralists, whose legacy spread across Eurasia.
overview.EAS_SNT_YAK: The Yakuts, or Sakha, are an ethnic group originating from Siberia, particularly in the Yakutia region, a vast area located in the far east of Russia. Their ancestors were Turkic-speaking horse breeders who migrated north from the Lake Baikal region several centuries ago. Upon reaching the Lena River basin, these migrants encountered and mixed with the indigenous hunter-gatherer peoples who already inhabited the taiga, mainly of Tungusic origin, in a fusion process that is key to Yakut ethnogenesis. This process resulted in a genetic profile that combines the heritage of their southern migrant ancestors with a significant substrate from the native peoples of northeastern Siberia.
overview.EUR_BRT_GBR: United Kingdom, located in northwestern Europe, genetically reflects the various layers of successive migrations that populated the islands. The oldest and most widespread genetic substrate comes from Bronze Age peoples who spoke Celtic languages, descendants of a mixture of the region`s first farmers and migrants from the steppe. The most transformative event was the arrival of the Anglo-Saxon peoples after the fall of Rome, whose massive settlement in the south and east of the territory created the genetic and linguistic core of what would become England. Therefore, a high Germanic component can be observed in England. Later, during the Viking era, Norse settlements added another significant layer, so strong that in the Orkney Islands Norwegian ancestry can reach up to 25%.
overview.EUR_BRT_IRL: Ireland, the third-largest island in Europe, has its roots in the early Neolithic farmers who arrived on the island over 5,500 years ago, the builders of the great megalithic monuments such as Newgrange. Subsequently, during the Bronze Age, the arrival of new peoples from the continent, bearers of Celtic languages and steppe ancestry, merged with this Neolithic population to forge the nucleus of the Irish people. To this ancient substrate, later layers were added during the Viking Age, when the Norse founded Ireland`s first cities such as Dublin, and later with the arrival of the Anglo-Normans.
overview.EUR_EST_BLR: Belarusian ancestry was shaped in the heart of Eastern Europe, from East Slavic tribes, such as the Dregovichs and Krivichs, who settled in the region during the Early Middle Ages. A key element in their genetic history is the profound interaction and admixture with Baltic peoples who previously inhabited these lands. This fusion of Slavic and Baltic roots shaped a genetic profile that is often positioned as a bridge between Baltic populations, such as Lithuanians and Latvians, and other East Slavic populations, such as Russians and Ukrainians. The region`s long history within multi-ethnic entities, such as the Grand Duchy of Lithuania, also facilitated continued genetic exchange with its neighbors.
overview.EUR_EST_CZE: The Czech Republic, located in the heart of Europe, rests on a deep history beginning with ancient Celtic tribes such as the Boii, who gave their name to Bohemia. Its fundamental pillar was established with the arrival of West Slavic peoples from the 6th century onwards, who laid the linguistic and cultural foundations of the region. Due to its central position and long history within entities such as the Holy Roman Empire and the Austrian Empire, there was prolonged contact and significant intermingling with German-speaking populations. This historical process has shaped a genetic profile that acts as a bridge between Central and Eastern Europe, fusing a Slavic core with significant Germanic influences and vestiges of an older Celtic past.
overview.EUR_EST_EST: In northeastern Europe, Estonia presents a genetic profile initially linked to its ancient Finno-Ugric roots. Its history originates in ancient migrations of peoples who spoke Uralic languages and settled on the Baltic coast millennia ago, preserving a distinctive genetic profile in the region. Over the centuries, contact and admixture with neighboring Baltic, Germanic, and Slavic peoples added additional layers to its genetic pool. As a result, the Estonian genetic heritage is a combination of an ancestral Finno-Ugric base with influences from the broader genetic mosaic of northern and eastern Europe.
overview.EUR_EST_HUN: Hungary tells a unique story in the heart of Europe, that of a people whose language has its roots in the distant Ural Mountains, but whose genetics are deeply integrated into the mosaic of Europe. This story began in the 9th century with the arrival of the Magyar tribes, a people of nomadic, Uralic-speaking horsemen who conquered the Pannonian Plain. These conquerors were a minority who mixed with the much larger local population, composed mainly of Slavic peoples, who eventually adopted the Magyar language and identity. Therefore, the Hungarian genetic profile is primarily that of its Slavic and Germanic neighbors, but enriched with a distinct founder lineage that connects its history to the Eurasian steppe and its ancient Uralic origins.
overview.EUR_EST_LTU: Lithuania offers a unique window into Bronze Age Europe. Its language, Lithuanian, is considered by linguists to be the most archaic living Indo-European language, and this remarkable preservation parallels its genetic history. The Lithuanian ancestral profile was shaped primarily by the fusion of ancient hunter-gatherers from northeastern Europe with the arrival of Indo-European-speaking steppe peoples around 4,500 years ago. Following this founder admixture, the Baltic peoples lived in relative isolation in the forests and coasts of the Baltic Sea, which protected them from the major migrations that transformed the rest of the continent, allowing both their language and genetic heritage to remain more stable.
overview.EUR_EST_LVA: Latvia traces its deepest roots to the ancient Baltic tribes that settled on the eastern coast of the Baltic Sea about 4,000 years ago. Upon arriving, these peoples encountered and mingled with Finno-Ugric speaking communities, such as the Livonians, a pivotal interaction that has left a lasting imprint on Latvia`s cultural and genetic heritage. This legacy is reflected in the Latvian language, which belongs to one of the oldest and most conservative branches of the Indo-European language family. Throughout its history, Latvia`s position as a northern European crossroads also fostered genetic exchanges with neighboring Germanic and Slavic peoples, adding further layers to the region`s ancestral profile.
overview.EUR_EST_POL: Poland lies at the geographic and genetic heart of the West Slavic peoples. This region of the North European Plain has been a stage of remarkable population continuity since the Bronze Age, with a heritage tracing back to a mixture of early European farmers and Indo-European-speaking steppe migrants. It was from this ancient base that, in the Early Middle Ages, West Slavic tribes, such as the Polans and Vistulans, expanded and consolidated, giving rise to the Polish people and state.
overview.EUR_EST_RUS: The ancestral history of the Russian people begins with the great expansion of Slavic tribes into the vast forests and plains of Eastern Europe during the Early Middle Ages. As they expanded north and east, these Slavic farmers encountered and extensively mixed with the Finno-Ugric-speaking peoples inhabiting the region, a fusion that is fundamental in the ethnogenesis of the Russians. This East Slavic heritage is shared with the Belarusian and Ukrainian peoples, with whom the Russians have a common origin in Kievan Rus`, founded after the arrival of the Varangians (Norsemen). Furthermore, it shows a strong genetic affinity with its Western Slavic neighbors, such as the Poles, due to their shared roots in the Early Middle Ages Slavic migrations.
overview.EUR_EST_SVK: The genetic profile of Slovakia emerges from the West Slavic tribes who settled in the heart of Europe, in the mountainous Carpathian region, during the Early Middle Ages. Its early history is closely linked to that of the Czech people, with whom their ancestors formed the first large Slavic state in the region, Great Moravia, in the 9th century. Following the fall of Great Moravia, Slovak history unfolded for almost a millennium in coexistence with the Hungarian people, an interaction that also left its mark on the cultural and genetic landscape. Therefore, the Slovak genetic profile unites a strong West Slavic core with a complex history of influences from its Hungarian and Germanic neighbors.
overview.EUR_EST_SVN: Slovenia lies at one of Europe’s key crossroads, at the point where the Slavic, Germanic, and Latin worlds meet at the easternmost end of the Alps. The history of this people begins in the 6th century CE, when South Slavic-speaking tribes migrated into this region, introducing the language that would become Slovene. These newcomers did not find an empty land, but instead mixed with the pre-existing population, a substrate of Celtic peoples who had been Romanized for centuries. The Slovenian genetic profile thus features a Slavic core but shares deep affinities with its Italian and Austrian neighbors, reflecting a millennia-long history of interaction in the heart of Europe.
overview.EUR_EST_UKR: The territory of Ukraine was the center of Kyivan Rus`, the first major medieval Slavic state, from which the Ukrainian, Belarusian, and Russian peoples originated. After the fall of Kyivan Rus`, the ancestors of Ukrainians forged a distinct history, marked by centuries of interaction with their western neighbors, especially within the Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth. At the same time, their position in the Pontic steppe fostered a unique frontier culture, best exemplified by the Cossacks. Therefore, the Ukrainian genetic profile represents a foundational Eastern Slavic lineage, forged in the cradle of Kyivan Rus` and shaped by the interaction between Central Europe and the Eurasian steppe.
overview.EUR_FRG_AUT: Austria, situated in the heart of Europe, builds its genetic origin upon an ancient base of Celtic peoples from the Iron Age (Hallstatt and La Tène cultures) which was later integrated into the Roman Empire. After the fall of Rome, the region was repopulated and transformed by the arrival of Germanic peoples, such as the Bavarians from the west, and Slavic peoples from the east, who mixed with the local population. This process of superposition of layers—Germanic and Slavic over a Celto-Roman substrate—is what defines the rich genetic mosaic of present-day Austria, connecting it with all the great families of peoples in Central Europe.
overview.EUR_FRG_BEL: Belgium and Luxembourg lie at the heart of Europe. Their deepest genetic roots are found in ancient Celtic tribes, such as the Belgae, who inhabited these lands before their integration into the Roman Empire. A transformative event was the subsequent migration of Germanic peoples, especially the Franks, whose settlement decisively influenced the genetic landscape and gave rise to the historical linguistic division of the area. This complex heritage, enriched by centuries of exchanges with neighboring populations, creates a genetic profile that links with the heritages of France and Germany, uniting Celtic, Roman, and Germanic legacies.
overview.EUR_FRG_CHE: Switzerland`s genetic profile is the result of its position as an Alpine crossing point for the great migrations that shaped Central Europe. The base of its population is a Celtic substratum, formed by tribes such as the Helvetii, which was later integrated into the Roman Empire. After the fall of Rome, the arrival of various Germanic peoples shaped the linguistic and genetic map of the country: the Alemanni settled in the north (the origin of German-speaking Switzerland), the Burgundians in the west (French-speaking Switzerland), and the Lombards in the south (Italian-speaking Switzerland). For this reason, Swiss ancestry is a mosaic that reflects this history of superposition, uniting its diverse communities through a common Celto-Roman heritage and subsequent Germanic diversification.
overview.EUR_FRG_DEU: Germany, located in the center of Europe, reflects a history of convergence between the three major groups of peoples of the continent: Celts, Germans, and Slavs. The oldest substrate of the region is Celtic, especially in the south and west, a population that was deeply influenced by the Roman Empire along the borders of the Rhine and the Danube. Subsequently, the genetic history of Germany was defined by two major movements in the High Middle Ages: the expansion of Germanic tribes throughout the territory, and the settlement of Slavic peoples in the east of the country. Therefore, the German genetic profile is a complex mosaic that reflects this history: a predominantly Germanic core superimposed on a Celtic base and with a significant Slavic contribution in the east, uniting Germany with all its neighboring populations.
overview.EUR_FRG_FRA: In France, three major peoples converged in ancient Gaul: Celts, Romans, and Germans. The fundamental genetic and cultural substrate comes from the Gauls, a group of Celtic peoples who, after the Roman conquest, adopted the Latin language and culture, giving rise to a prosperous Gallo-Roman civilization. After the fall of Rome, the arrival of the Franks, a Germanic people, was a key event; although they were a minority, they established the kingdom that would give its name to France and added an important genetic component, especially in the north. In this way, a genetic gradient was created from north to south: the north, more influenced by this Germanic layer, and the south, which retained a more markedly Mediterranean profile. Therefore, the French genetic profile is a mosaic of this triple heritage: a Celtic base, unified by Roman civilization, and enriched by subsequent Germanic influence.
overview.EUR_FRG_NLD: The Netherlands is located in the delta of the major rivers of northwestern Europe, a border region where the Roman world met the Germanic peoples. The genetic profile of the Dutch people is a mosaic of three major West Germanic tribes who settled the region after the fall of Rome: the Franks in the south, the Saxons in the east, and the Frisians along the coast. The Frisians especially shared deep ties with other North Sea peoples such as the Anglo-Saxons and Scandinavians, a connection that was reinforced during the Viking Age. Therefore, Dutch genetics is a convergence of these diverse Germanic branches, creating a genetic profile that bridges Germany, Scandinavia, and the British Isles.
overview.EUR_GRB_ALB: Albania, located in the western Balkan Peninsula, is home to a people whose ancestry offers a unique window into the ancient world of the region. Their language, Albanian, is an isolated Indo-European branch, and their genetic profile reflects this uniqueness, largely descending from the Paleo-Balkan peoples of the Iron Age, such as the Illyrians. While Slavic migrations transformed their neighbors, the ancestors of the Albanians preserved their language and identity in their mountainous refuge. However, the subsequent centuries of history within the Roman, Byzantine, and especially the Ottoman empires fostered interaction and left a genetic imprint of other Balkan and Anatolian peoples.
overview.EUR_GRB_BGR: Bulgaria, located on the Balkan Peninsula, has a genetic profile resulting from the mixture of three main ancestral components: a paleo-Balkan substrate, Slavic migrants, and Proto-Bulgarians. The indigenous population of the region, composed of peoples such as the Thracians, was demographically overtaken and assimilated by Slavic tribes that migrated to the Balkans from the 6th century onwards. In the 7th century, a group of Proto-Bulgarians of Turkic steppe origin established political control, but their genetic impact was limited as they were numerically minor and ended up being linguistically Slavicized by the majority population. Consequently, current Bulgarian genetics are predominantly a mixture of Slavic and paleo-Balkan ancestry, with a minor contribution from Central Asia, which firmly places them within the genetic spectrum of the Balkans.
overview.EUR_GRB_BIH: Bosnia and Herzegovina lies at the heart of the Western Balkans and represents the shared heritage of the South Slavic peoples who have inhabited this mountainous region for nearly 1,500 years. The genetic profile of the region was forged in the High Middle Ages, when Slavic tribes migrating to the Balkans mixed with pre-existing Paleo-Balkan peoples, such as the Illyrians, who had been previously influenced by Rome. This ancestral origin is common to the three constituent peoples of Bosnia and Herzegovina—Bosniaks, Serbs and Croats—who, despite their distinct religious and cultural identities, are genetically very similar. Subsequent Ottoman and Austro-Hungarian eras introduced profound cultural influences, but the genetic foundation of the population, rooted in that Slavic and Paleo-Balkan fusion, has remained as a testament to their shared history.
overview.EUR_GRB_GRC: Greece, in the southern Balkans, is home to a people whose ancestry is deeply rooted in the Bronze Age civilizations of the Aegean Sea. Their genetic profile was forged from the merging of two major prehistoric groups: the early Neolithic farmers of Anatolia, who created the Minoan civilization, and a later wave of steppe peoples who helped shape the Mycenaean civilization. Ancient DNA studies demonstrate that modern Greeks are largely direct descendants of these Mycenaean peoples, retaining the core of their heritage through the Classical, Roman, and Byzantine periods. Added to this ancient lineage were later influences, such as from Slavic migrations into the Balkans, which added further layers to the genetic mosaic of the region.
overview.EUR_GRB_HRV: Croatia lies at the crossroads of the Adriatic, where Slavic migrations met the ancient Mediterranean world of the Western Balkans. The deepest genetic substrate of the region stems from Paleo-Balkan peoples, such as the Illyrian tribes, who were deeply influenced by Roman civilization, creating a flourishing Illyro-Roman culture along the Dalmatian coast. In the 7th century, the arrival of South Slavic tribes transformed the region; these new settlers mixed with the local population, in a process of fusion that gave rise to the Croatian people and language. Thus, the Croatian genetic profile combines a Slavic core with a strong Paleo-Balkan base and subsequent Venetian and Central European influences.
overview.EUR_GRB_MDA: The genetic profile of Moldova is intimately linked to the history of the Eastern Balkan Romance-speaking peoples, sharing a very similar ancestral and linguistic origin with neighboring Romania.
