This lineage was forged from the fusion of the Daco-Roman peoples—descendants of the ancient Dacians and the settlers of the Roman Empire—with the Slavic tribes that migrated to the region during the Early Middle Ages. This mixture gave rise to the Vlach peoples, who in the 14th century founded the historical Principality of Moldova, developing a rich history at the crossroads of the great empires. Therefore, the Moldovan genetic profile is a Balkan mosaic, defined by its Daco-Roman substrate and a strong Slavic layer, a testament to the shared history that unites the peoples on both sides of the Prut River.
overview.EUR_GRB_MKD: The ancestry of North Macedonia is primarily linked to Slavic peoples who migrated to the Balkan Peninsula during the Early Middle Ages. These groups settled in the region, mixing with the pre-existing Paleo-Balkan populations. The long era of the Byzantine and Ottoman empires was another key factor that shaped the genetic mosaic of the area, fostering deep interaction with neighboring Greek, Albanian, and other Balkan peoples. As a result, the genetic heritage of North Macedonia is a profile that combines a Slavic core with a complex overlay of ancient and medieval influences.
overview.EUR_GRB_ROM: Romania is a country located on the Balkan Peninsula, bordered by Hungary and Serbia to the west, Ukraine and Moldova to the northeast and east, and Bulgaria to the south. It has approximately 19 million inhabitants and its official language is Romanian, a Romance language very similar to Italian.  During the Middle Ages, Romania was a key territory in the conflicts between Asia and Europe, experiencing continuous episodes of settlement and migration. Moreover, given its geographical location, Romania is the meeting point of the genetic history of Central Europe, Eastern Europe and the Balkan Peninsula. For these reasons, not only European genetic variants are found in Romanian genetics, but there are also very characteristic genetic markers in the Middle East.
overview.EUR_GRB_ROU: Romania is home to a people that represents an "island of Latinity" in Eastern Europe, with an ancestry forged from the fusion of ancient Dacian tribes with settlers of the Roman Empire. This Daco-Roman heritage gave rise to the Romanian language, a Romance language that was preserved in a region surrounded by Slavic and Hungarian-speaking peoples. Following the Roman withdrawal, migrations of Slavic peoples introduced a significant genetic and cultural influence, integrating with the local population and shaping its Balkan profile. Subsequent interactions with Hungarian, Greek neighbors, and the dynamics of the Ottoman Empire added additional layers, creating the current Romanian genetic profile: a Balkan mosaic with a Daco-Roman core and a strong Slavic imprint.
overview.EUR_IBS_BSQ: The Basque people are distinguished by a unique cultural identity and a language, Basque, which represents an echo of the languages spoken in Western Europe before the arrival of Indo-European peoples. This linguistic singularity is reflected in their genetic profile, which shows an extraordinary continuity with the first Neolithic farmers who settled in the Iberian Peninsula about 7,500 years ago. The relative isolation of their ancestors in the valleys of the western Pyrenees meant that they received a considerably smaller genetic impact from the later migrations of the Bronze and Iron Ages that transformed the rest of the region. Therefore, the Basque people offer a unique window into the genetic and linguistic diversity of the continent before the great expansions that shaped most of today`s European populations.
overview.EUR_IBS_ESP: Spain, located on the Iberian Peninsula, shows a very rich genetic profile, formed by millennia of migrations that turned the Iberian Peninsula into a crossroads between Europe, Africa, and the Mediterranean. Its genetic foundation, shared with the rest of Iberia, was forged in prehistory, from the deep mixing between the first Neolithic farmers and a subsequent wave of Indo-European speaking steppe migrants in the Bronze Age, giving rise to the Celtic and Iberian peoples. On this basis, the Roman Empire culturally unified the peninsula, but it was two great subsequent migrations that added the final and distinctive layers: the arrival of the Germanic Visigoths and the influence of North African peoples during the centuries of Al-Andalus history. This complex legacy is what defines Spain's genetic profile.
overview.EUR_IBS_PRT: Portugal, on the Atlantic façade of the Iberian Peninsula, is home to a people whose ancestral history was shaped by its position as a bridge between Europe and North Africa. Their genetic foundation, shared with the rest of Iberia, is a deep mixture of the first Neolithic farmers with the Bronze Age peoples who arrived from the steppe, giving rise to ancient peoples such as the Lusitanians. Following Romanization, their unique profile was defined by two major medieval movements: the establishment of the Germanic kingdom of the Suebi in the north, and the genetic influence from North Africa, which occurred in the south during the Al-Andalus period. This complex legacy is what defines Portugal's genetic profile.
overview.EUR_ITL: Italy is located in the south of Europe and in the center of the Mediterranean Sea. It is estimated that its population is made up of approximately 60 million individuals. For centuries this country has been an area of settlement of different populations, highlighting the Celts in the north and the Greeks in the south of the territory. This unequal settlement of the first settlers has been the cause of the current genetic differentiation of Italians throughout the peninsula. The southern Italians are genetically similar to the Greeks, while the northern Italians are closer to the Spanish and French in the south.
overview.EUR_ITL_CSS: These regions of Italy tell the story of a melting pot of peoples in the heart of the Mediterranean. Their genetic foundations lie in the ancient Italic tribes and, fundamentally, in the extensive Greek colonization (Magna Graecia), which left a Hellenic imprint on the region. Unification under Rome and subsequent rule by the Byzantine Empire (Eastern Roman Empire) strengthened the region`s ties to the Eastern Mediterranean for over a millennium. Arab conquests in Sicily and subsequent Norman invasions introduced additional genetic influences from North Africa, the Middle East, and Northern Europe, ultimately shaping this rich ancestral mosaic.
overview.EUR_ITL_NTH: Northern Italy reflects its history as a bridge between continental Europe and the Mediterranean world. Its oldest roots lie in peoples such as the Ligurians and, prominently, in various Celtic tribes (Gauls), whose genetic legacy connects the region with populations on the other side of the Alps. Conquest and colonization by Rome overlaid a profound Latin influence, integrating the Po Valley into the heart of the Empire. Subsequently, the migrations of Germanic peoples during the Early Middle Ages, especially that of the Lombards who established their kingdom here, added another key ancestral layer that has contributed genetically to northern Italy.
overview.EUR_NTH_FIN: Finland narrates on a genetic level the history of the Uralic-speaking peoples of Fennoscandia, starting with an ancient migration of Uralic origin from the east that brought a characteristic Siberian genetic component and mixed with the early inhabitants of the region: Stone Age hunter-gatherer peoples. From this common ancestral root, the lineage split: the Sami adapted to life in the Arctic regions, while the ancestors of the Finns settled further south. The Finnish population, in particular, was formed from a relatively small number of these founders, which, coupled with prolonged isolation, created a very particular initial genetic landscape. Superimposed on this unique foundation was the most significant influence of its later history: the more than 600 years of belonging to the Kingdom of Sweden, which introduced a significant layer of Scandinavian ancestry. Therefore, modern Finnish heritage is a fascinating mosaic: a Uralic-Siberian core that distinguishes it from Scandinavia, combined with a substantial Scandinavian heritage that differentiates it from other Eastern European populations with the same Uralic origin.
overview.EUR_NTH_PES: Scandinavia is a region located in Northern Europe, including Denmark, Sweden, and Norway, but the Scandinavian world extends culturally and genetically to other regions such as Iceland. The genetic profile of Scandinavian populations is the result of the mixing of three major prehistoric groups: a substrate of local hunter-gatherers, a later migration of Neolithic farmers, and a significant contribution from Bronze Age steppe peoples, whose fusion gave rise to the North Germanic peoples. During the Viking Age (8th-11th centuries), a notable demographic expansion took place, involving not only the emigration of Scandinavians, but also the arrival of new lineages, as demonstrated by the founding of Iceland by a mixed population of Norse men and women of Gaelic origin. Additionally, there has been continuous contact and localized mixing with Uralic-speaking peoples in the northern regions. Consequently, Scandinavian ancestry is defined by a North Germanic core with detectable contributions from insular and continental Europe.
overview.OCE_PAP: The Papuan people are the native ethnic group of Papua New Guinea, Oceania. The country of Papua New Guinea comprises the eastern half of the island of New Guinea, as well as numerous surrounding islands. Papua New Guinea has been populated since ancient times, with the oldest archaeological remains found in the area dating back 60,000 years. Genetically, very little is known about these early settlers, although it is believed that they may have come from Southeast Asia.
overview.OCE_PAP_HIG: In the Highlands of Papua New Guinea, we find peoples who have inhabited the isolated mountain valleys of the island`s interior for tens of thousands of years. Although they descend, like all Papuans, from the first humans to leave Africa approximately 50,000 years ago, their subsequent history was shaped by profound geographical isolation. This millennia-long isolation has created a unique and very stable genetic profile; indeed, the Highlanders were among the world`s first independent farmers, an event that shaped their society. Therefore, their genetic heritage is an extraordinary window into the history of the early inhabitants of the ancient continent of Sahul and the development of agriculture in one of the planet`s most remote environments.
overview.OCE_PAP_SEP: The inhabitants of the Sepik River basin form a vibrant mosaic of lowland peoples of Papua New Guinea, such as the Iatmul, Abelam, or Chambri, whose culture is linked to the island`s largest river network. Like all Papuans, their ancestors were part of one of the first human migrations out of Africa more than 50,000 years ago. Genetically, the Sepik peoples reflect a history of dynamism, where the river has acted as a channel for cultural and genetic exchange between different groups. This rich history of interaction is manifested in their extraordinary artistic and ritual diversity, such as in the elaborate carvings of their spirit houses (`haus tambaran`) that celebrate their clans` relationship with the river.
overview.WAS_DRZ: The Druze are a religious and cultural community originating in the Levant, with a majority presence in Lebanon, Syria, and Israel, and smaller communities in Jordan. Emerging in the 11th century from Ismaili Islam, they have developed a unique faith incorporating Neoplatonic, Gnostic, Christian, and other systems of thought. Traditionally settled in mountainous regions, they have preserved a cohesive identity, with strong community ties and a social structure based on solidarity, education, and respect for ancestral wisdom. Although numerically small, the Druze have significantly contributed to the history and cultural diversity of the Middle East.
overview.WAS_ELA_ARP: The Arabian Peninsula has been for millennia a prosperous crossroads for ancient civilizations and trade routes. Before the Islamic era, this area saw kingdoms and city-states flourish in its oases and coasts, such as the Dilmun and Magan civilizations, or the kingdoms of southern Arabia, which prospered thanks to the trade of incense and spices. The region's genetic profile often reflects this history of interaction, showing connections not only within the peninsula but also with the Near East, Persia, and South Asia. Although distinct from the more nomadic Bedouin populations of the interior, these sedentary communities have always maintained a close relationship with them, in a dynamic of exchange that has shaped Arab identity over the centuries.
overview.WAS_ELA_EGY: Egypt is inextricably linked to the Nile River, which created a fertile corridor in Northeast Africa and was the cradle of one of history's most enduring civilizations. Ancient DNA studies reveal that the inhabitants of Pharaonic Egypt possessed a primarily Near Eastern genetic profile, with closer ties to Levantine populations. Over the centuries, this ancient genetic substratum was enriched with additional influences; an increase in Sub-Saharan ancestry is observed, especially in the south due to its connection with Nubia, as well as a contribution from the Arabian Peninsula after the 7th-century expansion. Therefore, modern Egyptians are the direct heirs of ancient Egyptians, carriers of a complex genetic mosaic that narrates the millennia-long history of interactions between Africa and the Mediterranean along the Nile.
overview.WAS_ELA_JMZ: Mizrahi Jews are descendants of Jewish communities that have historically inhabited the Middle East, North Africa, and Central Asia, in countries such as Iraq, Iran, Yemen, Syria, or Morocco. The word Mizrahi is a sociological term meaning "eastern" in Hebrew. Although these communities did not form a unified group until World War II, they share a rich and diverse cultural heritage, shaped by the languages, music, and customs of their regions of origin. Over time, they have developed distinctive religious and social practices within Judaism. They should not be confused with Sephardi Jews, as they are not descended from communities originating in the Iberian Peninsula.
overview.WAS_ELA_JSM: Sephardic Jews are a historical community with deep roots in the Iberian Peninsula, especially in what are now Spain and Portugal, where they lived until their expulsion in 1492. Following this exile, at the end of the 15th century, many settled in North Africa, particularly in Morocco, where Moroccan Jews had already lived since 70 AD. From 1492 onwards, Moroccan Judaism predominantly adopted the Sephardic liturgy, consolidating a unique cultural identity. Among its most prominent cultural aspects are Ladino, a Romance language derived from old Spanish and Portuguese, and Haketia, a variety of Judeo-Spanish influenced by Arabic. A prominent figure in this community is the writer and philosopher André Azoulay.
overview.WAS_ELA_LEV: The Levantine region includes some of the most vital areas in history, the eastern Mediterranean coastal strip that today encompasses Lebanon, Syria, Jordan, Palestine, and Israel. The genetic profile of this region shows a connection to its Bronze Age inhabitants, the Canaanites, who established the first great city-states in the area. It was their descendants, the Phoenicians, who created and disseminated the first alphabet—a legacy that transformed the world—while the Aramean kingdoms flourished inland. Over the centuries, subsequent influences from Greeks, Romans, Arabs, and other peoples added layers to this ancient Canaanite substrate, forging the rich and complex genetic mosaic of modern Levantine populations.
overview.WAS_ELA_SAM: The Samaritans are an ethnic and religious group with ancestral roots in the Samaria region, in the modern-day Near East. Their history dates back to ancient times, and they maintain their own identity based on particular religious traditions and a liturgical language derived from ancient Hebrew. Over the centuries, the Samaritans have preserved their customs, calendars, and sacred texts with great fidelity. Today, they represent a valuable living testament to the ancient cultures of the Levant.
overview.WAS_ELA_YJW: Yemenite Jews are one of the oldest and most unique communities in the Jewish diaspora, whose history unfolded over more than two millennia in the southern Arabian Peninsula. Their genetic profile clearly narrates this history: it combines an ancestral Middle Eastern core, connecting them to other Jewish and Levantine groups, with a distinctive influence from ancient Arabian Peninsula lineages. Due to a long history of endogamy (marriage within the community), Yemenite Jews became a `genetic isolate`, preserving this mixture and differentiating themselves over time from both other Jewish populations and their Yemeni neighbors. Therefore, this heritage represents a genetic bridge between the Levant and Arabia.
overview.WAS_TCI: Located in Western Asia are Turkey, Iran and the Caucasus, home to more than 183 million people. It is one of the regions with the most ethnic groups in the world, and they all share a common genetic history. The origin of these populations was marked by the settlement of the Mongols in the 13th century, which resulted in a high number of migrations to neighboring territories. For this reason, the genetics of Turks, Caucasians and Iranians present genetic markers common to southern European populations, especially in the case of southern Italians.
overview.WAS_TCI_ARM: Armenia tells a story of continuity in the Armenian Highland, the mountainous crossroads between Anatolia, the Caucasus, and Mesopotamia. Studies suggest that the genetic profile of the population of the territory of Armenia emerged during the Bronze Age, more than 3,000 years ago, and has remained remarkably stable since then. This genetic profile corresponds to their language, Armenian, which constitutes an independent and early branch within the Indo-European family. On this ancestral foundation, a distinct cultural identity was forged, marked by ancient kingdoms such as Urartu and by being the first nation to adopt Christianity. Throughout its history, the territory of Armenia was a vital nexus between great empires such as the Persian, Roman, Byzantine, and Ottoman, which fostered centuries of genetic and cultural interaction.
overview.WAS_TCI_AZE: Azerbaijan is located at the crossroads of Eastern Europe and Western Asia, bordered by the Caspian Sea and the Caucasus Mountains. The region's oldest genetic substrate comes from native Caucasian peoples, such as the Caucasian Albanians, and ancient Iranian peoples who inhabited these lands for millennia. The transformative event in its history was the arrival of Oghuz Turkic tribes from Central Asia starting in the 11th century; their language was gradually adopted by the local population, shaping the modern Azerbaijani identity. As a result, the Azerbaijani genetic profile is a complex mosaic, predominantly Caucasian and Iranian, but united by a Turkic language and cultural heritage, reflecting its history as a bridge between civilizations.
overview.WAS_TCI_GEO: Georgia, located at the crossroads of Europe and Asia, is a mountainous region of the Caucasus known for its impressive cultural diversity and natural landscapes. Its population possesses a rich historical heritage dating back to ancient kingdoms such as Colchis and Iberia, and its language, Georgian, belongs to a unique linguistic family. Georgian culture stands out for its hospitality, polyphonic music, and culinary tradition. Genetically, the inhabitants of Georgia show a complex ancestral mixture, reflecting centuries of exchanges between peoples of the Caucasus, Anatolia, and the Near East. This diversity is an essential part of their identity.
overview.WAS_TCI_NEC: The Northeast Caucasus is a region of imposing mountains and valleys known as the "mountain of languages" for its incredible linguistic diversity, with its epicenter in Dagestan. Here, peoples such as the Lezgins, who speak a native Caucasian language, and the Kumyks, who speak a language of the Turkic family, coexist. Despite this difference, peoples who might seem distinct share an overwhelming common ancestral substrate, derived from the oldest lineages of the Caucasus region. The history of the Kumyks is a clear example of linguistic replacement, where a local Caucasian population adopted the language of a Turkic elite, a pattern of genetic continuity under diverse cultural layers that defines the rich ancestral tapestry of this region.
overview.WAS_TCI_NWC: In the heart of the North Caucasus lies a mountainous region that has served as a refuge for ancient human lineages and today is home to an extraordinary mosaic of peoples. Despite the astonishing diversity of their languages, peoples such as the Adyghe, Kabardians, Chechens, Ossetians, and Balkars share a deep common genetic base, rooted in the ancient hunter-gatherers and early farmers of the Caucasus. This linguistic diversity is the result of a complex history, in which various later migrations, such as that of the Alans (ancestors of the Ossetians) from the steppe, introduced their languages, which were adopted by genetically local populations. Therefore, this genetic profile tells a fascinating story of resilience, where an ancient Caucasian substrate has endured, integrating new cultural and linguistic layers over millennia.
overview.WAS_TCI_PME: In the heart of the Mesopotamian region and the Iranian Plateau, humanity enacted the agricultural revolution and founded the first cities in the world. The genetic profile of this area originates from the first Neolithic farmers, whose lineages expanded and mixed here more than 10,000 years ago. It was these peoples who gave life to civilizations such as Sumer, Akkad, Babylonia, and Elam, and who later formed the nucleus of the great Persian empires that connected East and West. Therefore, despite the diverse languages and cultures that exist today in the regions of Iran and Iraq, their genetic profile shows the connection of their modern peoples to the dawn of civilization.
overview.WAS_TCI_TUR: The population of Turkey reflects the history of Anatolia itself: a melting pot where ancient Anatolian peoples merged with migrant Turkic tribes from Central Asia. The main genetic foundation of the region was established over millennia by its native inhabitants, including the Bronze Age Hittites and the Hellenized populations of the Greek, Roman, and Byzantine eras. From the 11th century onwards, the arrival of the Seljuk Turks from Central Asia introduced the Turkic language and culture, which were gradually adopted by the diverse local population, initiating a new chapter in the region`s history. As a result, the modern Turkish genetic profile is predominantly Anatolian and Mediterranean, enriched with a significant Central Asian heritage, reflecting an identity forged by the convergence of civilizations.

poblation.overviewEUR_GRB_ROM: Romania is a country located in the Balkan Peninsula; it borders Hungary and Serbia to the west, Ukraine and Moldova to the northeast and east, and Bulgaria to the south. It has approximately 19 million inhabitants, and its official language is Romanian, a Romance language very similar to Italian. During the Middle Ages, Romania was a key territory in the conflicts between Asia and Europe, experiencing continuous episodes of settlements and migrations. Furthermore, given its geographical location, Romania is the meeting point of the genetic history of Central Europe, Eastern Europe, and the Balkan Peninsula. For these reasons, Romanian genetics not only contain European genetic variants, but also very characteristic genetic markers exist in the Middle East.

overview.EUR_FRG_BEL: Belgium is located in the northwest of the European continent, bordered to the north by the Netherlands, to the east by Germany and to the south by Luxembourg and France. For years, the Belgian territory was divided between France and Germany, states with which it shares much of its culture and genetics. However, the Belgian population also has genetic properties unique to southern Europe, derived mainly from the waves of migration after World War II and its geographic orientation.

overview.AFR_EKU: Unlike the more isolated hunter-gatherer lineages, East African populations reflect that this territory has been a millennial meeting point between peoples of Africa and Eurasia. Their most distinctive genetic characteristic is a deep fusion, which occurred thousands of years ago, between native African peoples and a major migration of farmers who returned to Africa from the Near East, an event that profoundly marked the populations of the Horn of Africa, such as the Ethiopians and Somalis. Superimposed on this unique substrate were other major African migrations: the expansion of Nilo-Saharan-speaking pastoralists from the Nile Valley, ancestors of peoples such as the Maasai, and the arrival of farmers from the Bantu Expansion from the west. Therefore, this ancestral profile represents a unique tapestry, woven with the threads of an ancient Afro-Eurasian mix and the subsequent dynamics of the great families of peoples of Africa, creating a diversity that distinguishes it from other regions of the continent.
overview.AFR_MGB: The Maghreb region, in North Africa, has its origin deeply rooted in the Imazighen (Berber) peoples, the indigenous inhabitants of the region since prehistoric times. The first major event that shaped its genetic profile was the arrival of Neolithic farmers from the Near East, whose descendants also populated southern Europe, establishing an ancient genetic link across the Mediterranean. Subsequently, the Arab expansion of the 7th century introduced a new and decisive genetic and cultural component from the Arabian Peninsula, merging with the local population. Therefore, the genetic profile associated with the Maghrebi region is a rich mosaic, with an indigenous Imazighen base transformed by these great migrations and enriched by millennia of trade across the Mediterranean and the Sahara.
overview.AFR_PSH: The term pygmy is used to designate individuals living in Central Africa, especially in the African equatorial forests. Together with the Bushmen or San, they are considered the oldest inhabitants of Africa. The high inbreeding of these tribes, together with their isolation in forests, has given them a very distinctive genetics with respect to the rest of African populations. As a curiosity, and as a result of such characteristic genetics, it is worth mentioning the short stature and short life expectancy (between 15-24 years) of the individuals that make up these tribes.
overview.AFR_PSH_KHS: San and Khoisan of Southern Africa
overview.AFR_WGS: The Senegambia region in West Africa was the cradle of great empires that flourished long before contact with Europe. The ancestry of the populations of this region is deeply linked to Mandé-speaking peoples, such as the Mandinka, who were the architects of influential states, including the vast and prosperous Mali Empire in medieval times. The genetic profile of this region was forged on a millennial foundation of West African farmers, whose successful adaptation and social organization enabled the development of these complex societies.
overview.AFR_WNG: In the heart of West Africa, the region where we now find Nigeria and Ghana was the cradle of some of the continent's most sophisticated urban and artistic societies, such as those of the Yoruba and Esan peoples. It is deeply linked to the great kingdoms and city-states that flourished here, such as the Oyo Empire and the Kingdom of Benin, and to the spiritual and artistic center of Ife, famous for its incomparable bronze art. The genetic profile of this region was forged on an ancient base of West African farmers, whose prosperity and dense population allowed for the emergence of these advanced political structures. Their profound cultural influence spread throughout the region and, through the diaspora, throughout the world.
overview.AFR_WSL: The coast of West Africa, in the region that today encompasses Sierra Leone and Liberia, is home to an area of forests and savannas that has been home to Mandé-speaking peoples for centuries. The ancestry of this region is strongly linked to the Mende people and their relatives, whose ancestors migrated to this coastal region from the interior, bringing with them their rich agricultural and social traditions. The genetic profile of this population was forged from the fusion of these Mandé migrants with the older indigenous groups of the forest region, in a history framed by the domestication of African rice.
overview.AMR_AMZ: This ancestry delves into the heart of the Amazon, connecting with the profound history of the original peoples who have inhabited this vast and diverse region for millennia. Peoples such as the Karitiana and the Suruí, from the Tupí language family in Brazil, present a history of relative isolation that has made their DNA a key scientific reference for understanding the ancestral history of the first settlers of South America. The genetic profile they represent is a pillar for identifying the heritage of the numerous peoples who adapted and expanded throughout the Amazon basin. Although each Amazonian people possesses a unique identity, they all share a deep ancestral connection that distinguishes them as one of the main branches in the genealogical tree of Native Americans.
overview.AMR_AND_QUE: Native people of Aymara and Quechua origin
overview.AMR_CST: This ancestry originates from the first peoples who populated Central and South America, and it tells one of humanity`s greatest migration stories. Genetic evidence confirms that their ancestors traveled from Northeast Asia, crossed the ancient Beringia land bridge during the last Ice Age, and rapidly expanded southward across the entire continent. Over millennia, this expansion gave rise to astonishing diversity, as different groups adapted to environments as varied as the jungles of Mesoamerica or the high peaks of the Andes. This profound legacy is visible not only in hundreds of Indigenous peoples such as the Maya, Quechua, and Wichí, but also in the independent development of great civilizations in these regions.
overview.AMR_CST_AND: The Andean populations inhabit the Andes mountain range, the longest mountain chain in the world, which crosses South America from north to south. Their ancestors, descendants of the first settlers who crossed the Bering Strait, established civilizations like the Inca, which left a deep genetic and cultural mark on the region. The Quechuas and Aymaras are two of the largest indigenous peoples of the Andean region, although they share a common ancestor with other indigenous groups in America, they have had a degree of genetic isolation due to the mountainous topography of the region. Their prolonged residence at high altitudes is also reflected in their biology, as many Andean populations possess unique genetic variants associated with greater efficiency in oxygen transport, a remarkable adaptation to life at extreme altitudes.
overview.AMR_NTH: Distributed throughout the northern part of the American continent, there is the population formed by the native Pima people. It is an ethnic group that currently has a few hundred inhabitants of purely Pima genetics. The first region inhabited by this people was western Canada, and from there it expanded to occupy a large part of the continent. Genetically, it has been verified that the Pima descend from the first inhabitants of the American continent, who came from eastern Asia.
overview.CSA_BBI: Between South and Southeast Asia lies a region encompassing Bengal, Bangladesh, and Northeast India, which has served as a bridge for ancient migrations. The oldest genetic substrate in this area was established by Austroasiatic-speaking peoples, considered among the region's earliest farmers. Superimposed on this foundation were two major migratory currents: the expansion of Indo-Aryan-speaking peoples from the west, which formed the core of the Bengali people, and migrations of Tibeto-Burman peoples from the north and east, who populated the hills of Northeast India. Therefore, this genetic profile represents a continuum that uniquely blends South Asian and East Asian ancestry, reflecting the region's history as an age-old meeting point.
overview.CSA_CAS: Central Asia is a territory with a large number of ethnic groups, distributed mainly in five countries: Kazakhstan, Kyrgyzstan, Tajikistan, Turkmenistan, and Uzbekistan. The origin of this population dates back to the Indo-Iranian and Turkic expansions that took place centuries ago. As a consequence, today Central Asians share a high degree of genetic similarity with the peoples of Western Asia.
overview.CSA_GUJ: Northwestern India, in the historic region of Gujarat, was a hub of the Indus Valley civilization and a gateway for subsequent migrations. The genetic profile of these people is the result of the fundamental admixture of two major ancestral groups: the indigenous inhabitants of the subcontinent, related to the Indus Valley builders, and the Indo-Iranian speaking herders who arrived from the Central Asian steppes in the Bronze Age. The proportion of these two ancestries varies among communities, but the steppe component is often prominent in groups such as the Brahmins. To this dual heritage is added the legacy of millennia of maritime trade along the coasts of the Arabian Sea, which further enriched the genetic and cultural tapestry of this region.
overview.CSA_PAF: Located in South Asia and with more than 235 million inhabitants, are Pakistan and Afghanistan. Due to their geographical location, they are a region that has been settled by a variety of peoples. For this reason, they are currently two countries with a high degree of genetic variability, inhabited by numerous ethnic groups. In Pakistan, the Pashtun, Sindhi, Punjabi or Baluchi populations stand out, while in Afghanistan, groups such as the Pashtuns, Yayikos, Hazaras, Uzbeks or Baluchis can be found.
overview.CSA_PUN: The state of Punjab, located in northwestern India, has acted as a major passageway for all major human invasions in the Indian subcontinent. The result has been the mixing of foreign genes with the local populations, resulting in a wide range of genetic diversity and influencing the genetic structure of the Punjab populations. Today the most representative ethnic group in the region are the Punjabis, a population highly influenced by Hindu, Buddhist and Islamic culture.
overview.CSA_SRI: Southern India and Sri Lanka is a region that harbors some of the oldest and most continuous genetic lineages in all of Eurasia. Its main ancestral component comes directly from the first modern humans who arrived in the Indian subcontinent tens of thousands of years ago, also known as South Asian hunter-gatherers. This lineage formed the basis of the Dravidian-speaking populations that have dominated the region for millennia. Unlike northern India, the genetic impact of later Indo-Aryan migrations from the Central Asian steppe was significantly less here, which helped to better preserve this ancestral profile.
overview.EAS_DAI: This group narrates one of the most important stories of human migration: the expansion of the first rice farmers from southern present-day China, which shaped the identity of almost all of Southeast Asia. A large wave of these farmers moved south by land, establishing the genetic foundations of many peoples of the Indochinese peninsula, such as the Vietnamese and Cambodians; present-day groups like the Dai are very representative of this lineage. Another branch crossed to Taiwan, from where their descendants, the Austronesian peoples, starred in an extraordinary maritime expansion that populated the Philippines, Malaysia, and Indonesia. Therefore, this genetic profile unifies hundreds of peoples, from the Igorot and Atayal to the Dai and Khmer, through a common heritage rooted in the agricultural revolution of East Asia.
overview.EAS_HAN: The Han people are an ethnic group originating in China. It is one of the largest ethnic groups in the world, numbering approximately 1.2 billion people (about 18% of the world's population). Several scientific studies suggest that the Hans are descendants of the Huaxia tribe, an ancient population with which they share most genetic markers. It is now a very genetically homogeneous population.
overview.EAS_JAP: The ancestry of the populations of the Japanese archipelago tells a story of fusion between an ancient hunter-gatherer people and a later wave of farmers from the Asian continent. The oldest genetic base was established by the Jōmon people, the indigenous inhabitants of the islands for millennia, whose genetic legacy is preserved in its greatest proportion in the Ainu people of northern Japan. Around 3,000 years ago, the arrival of the Yayoi people from the Korean Peninsula introduced rice cultivation and a new and dominant East Asian genetic component, which mixed deeply with the Jōmon population. Most present-day Japanese (the Yamato people) are the result of this ancient integration.
overview.EAS_KOR: The Korean Peninsula tells the story of the fusion of ancient hunter-gatherer peoples with a wave of migrant farmers. The deepest genetic substrate comes from lineages of Northeast Asia, related to the peoples of the Amur River basin, over which was superimposed the influence of the first millet farmers of the Liao River region. The mixing of these two groups during the Bronze Age was the key event in the formation of the Korean people. Subsequently, a history of relative geographic isolation resulted in low admixture with other populations, which has made the Korean genetic profile one of the most homogeneous in Asia today.
overview.EAS_MON: This group includes individuals of the Mongolian people and of the Oroquen, Xibe, Hezher and Daur ethnic groups, established in northern China. The Mongols are the majority ethnic group of this group and have approximately 10 million individuals, distributed in northern China, Mongolia and southern Asia. Given the common historical past shared by all these populations, they are closely related ethnic groups from a genetic point of view.
overview.EAS_SNT: Siberia is a region that extends across northern Asia and forms the most Asian part of Russia. It has approximately 40 million inhabitants, of which 10% are indigenous tribes. Among the most representative ethnic groups in the territory are the Yakut people, who share genetic similarities with other indigenous groups that inhabit the region, such as the Evenks and the Ulchi. In addition, and given the geographic location of these peoples, evolution has led to the prevalence of genetic modifications that have allowed them to adapt to extreme climates.
overview.EUR_ITL: Italy is located in the south of Europe and in the center of the Mediterranean Sea. It is estimated that its population is made up of approximately 60 million individuals. For centuries this country has been an area of settlement of different populations, highlighting the Celts in the north and the Greeks in the south of the territory. This unequal settlement of the first settlers has been the cause of the current genetic differentiation of Italians throughout the peninsula. The southern Italians are genetically similar to the Greeks, while the northern Italians are closer to the Spanish and French in the south.
overview.EUR_JEW: The genetic profile of Ashkenazi Jews tells the story of a diaspora community that formed in Central and Eastern Europe, but whose deepest roots lie in the ancient Iron Age peoples of the Levant. Their genetic profile reflects this dual heritage: an ancestral base from the Near East, shared with other Jewish populations, which mixed with the local European populations where their first communities settled. A key event in their history was a severe population bottleneck in the Middle Ages, where a group of as few as a few hundred founder individuals gave rise to the majority of the present-day Ashkenazi population.
overview.EUR_SRD: Sardinia is an island located in the center of the Mediterranean Sea, and it represents a direct genetic window into prehistoric Europe. Sardinians are considered by geneticists to be the closest present-day population to the first Neolithic farmers who expanded across Europe from Anatolia about 8,000 years ago. The reason for their uniqueness lies in their relative isolation; unlike mainland Europe, the island of Sardinia received minimal genetic impact from the massive Bronze Age steppe migrations that did transform the rest of the continent. This profound genetic continuity from the Neolithic period allowed for the flourishing of the enigmatic Nuragic civilization, builders of thousands of stone towers. Subsequently, the island was not completely isolated: later colonizers such as the Phoenicians and Romans, and later the maritime powers of Pisa and the Crown of Aragon, left their own genetic imprint, albeit more subtly and mainly in coastal regions. Therefore, Sardinian genetics tell a direct connection to the peoples who introduced agriculture to Europe, enriched by later layers of Mediterranean history.
overview.OCE_MEL: The Melanesia region tells the fascinating story of the encounter between two of the greatest human expansions in Oceania: the first inhabitants of the glacial era and the later Austronesian navigators. The oldest genetic substrate of these islands was established by the descendants of the first humans who colonized the ancient continent of Sahul more than 50,000 years ago, bearers of a unique Papuan heritage. About 3,500 years ago, a new wave of Austronesian-speaking migrants, associated with the Lapita archaeological culture, arrived in Melanesia from the west, bringing with them new technologies. The genetic profile of modern Melanesians is the direct result of the mixture between these newcomers and the pre-existing Papuan populations, creating a gradient of ancestry that is unique to each archipelago and tells the story of the peopling of the entire Pacific.
overview.OCE_PAP: The Papuan people are the native ethnic group of Papua New Guinea, Oceania. The country of Papua New Guinea comprises the eastern half of the island of New Guinea, as well as numerous surrounding islands. Papua New Guinea has been populated since ancient times, with the oldest archaeological remains found in the area dating back 60,000 years. Genetically, very little is known about these early settlers, although it is believed that they may have come from Southeast Asia.
overview.WAS_BED: Bedouin peoples, nomadic herders whose ancestral home is the vast deserts of the Arabian Peninsula and the Near East, tell a story that dates back thousands of years before the Islamic era. Genetically, Bedouins are of exceptional interest, as they are considered the most direct modern representatives of the ancient pastoralists and hunter-gatherers of Arabia. It was these desert tribes who, in the 7th century, carried the Arabic language and culture out of the peninsula, spreading their genetic component throughout North Africa and the Levant. Although all Bedouin groups share this deep Arabian root, their wide distribution has created diversity among them, with some groups showing affinities with neighboring populations.
overview.WAS_DRZ: The Druze comprise a unique ethno-religious community originating in the Levant, where they were established in the 11th century from a branch of Ismaili Islam. Shortly after its founding, the community closed its faith to new converts and established a strict practice of endogamy; this decision, maintained for nearly a thousand years, has made them one of the world's most distinctive "genetic isolates." As a result, their genetic profile is an extraordinary window into the populations of the Levant from the medieval era, showing remarkable homogeneity among Druze communities in different countries of the area, such as Syria or Lebanon. Therefore, this heritage tells a story of exceptional cohesion, in which the preservation of a unique cultural and religious identity has gone hand in hand with the conservation of a singular genetic lineage.
overview.WAS_ELA: This group includes the native peoples of the Nile Valley, the Mediterranean Levant, and the Arabian Peninsula. In antiquity, this vast region was home to distinct but interconnected foundational civilizations: that of Pharaonic Egypt on the Nile, that of the Canaanite and Israelite peoples in the Levant, and that of the ancient cultures of Arabia. The common genetic thread that unites all the peoples of this grouping—from Egypt and the Levant to Arabia—is their shared descent from the first Neolithic farmers who inhabited this area more than 10,000 years ago. Upon this ancient foundation, the distinct cultures of antiquity developed, such as those of Pharaonic Egypt and the Semitic peoples of the Levant. Subsequently, the Arab expansion of the 7th century added a new layer of genetic and linguistic heritage that further unified much of the region, creating a deep and continuous historical tapestry.
overview.WAS_JEW: The Ashkenazi Jewish population is a group that currently consists of approximately 10 million people. Their origin dates back some 2000 years, when their ancestors moved from Western Asia to Central Europe. Their history is marked by the persecution, genocides and Holocaust that took place during World War II. This historical event caused Jews to emigrate all over the world. At present, the most representative groups of Ashkenazis are distributed among the United States, Israel and the former Soviet Union. Within this community, consanguineous marriages are a common practice, so it has become a very characteristic and homogeneous population from the genetic point of view.
overview.WAS_TCI: Located in Western Asia are Turkey, Iran and the Caucasus, home to more than 183 million people. It is one of the regions with the most ethnic groups in the world, and they all share a common genetic history. The origin of these populations was marked by the settlement of the Mongols in the 13th century, which resulted in a high number of migrations to neighboring territories. For this reason, the genetics of Turks, Caucasians and Iranians present genetic markers common to southern European populations, especially in the case of southern Italians.
